Explaining Scripture, which is prophesying, is compared with speaking in tongues. This attracted more attention than a simple explanation of Scripture; it satisfied pride more, but promoted Christian charity less; it would not equally benefit the souls of people. What cannot be understood can never edify. No advantage can be gained from even the most excellent speeches if they are delivered in language that the listeners cannot speak or understand. Every ability or possession is valuable to the degree that it is useful. Even strong, spiritual affection must be guided by understanding, otherwise people will disgrace the truths they claim to promote.

Even an apostle could not edify unless he spoke so as to be understood by his listeners. To speak words that have no meaning to those who hear them is like speaking into the air. That cannot achieve the purpose of speaking if it has no meaning; in this case, speaker and listener are strangers to each other. All religious services should be conducted in Christian gatherings so that everyone can participate and benefit from them. Plain and easy language is most suitable for public worship and other religious exercises. Every true follower of Christ will prefer to do good to others than to gain a reputation for learning or eloquent speaking.

There cannot be agreement with prayers that are not understood. A truly Christian minister will seek much more to benefit people’s souls spiritually than to receive the greatest praise himself. This proves he is a servant of Christ. Children are easily impressed by novelty; but do not act like them. Christians should be like children, without deceit or malice; yet they should not be unskilled in what concerns righteousness, but only in deceptive practices. It shows that a people have been abandoned by God when he allows them to be ruled by those who teach them to worship in an unfamiliar language. They cannot possibly benefit from such teaching. Yet this is how the preachers did who gave their instructions in an unknown tongue. Would it not make Christianity seem ridiculous to a non-Christian to hear ministers pray or preach in a language that neither he nor the assembly understands? But if those who minister plainly interpret Scripture, or preach the great truths and rules of the gospel, a non-Christian or uneducated person might become a Christian. His conscience might be touched, the secrets of his heart might be revealed to him, and so he might be brought to confess his guilt and acknowledge that God was present in the assembly. Scriptural truth, plainly and properly taught, has a wonderful power to awaken the conscience and touch the heart.

Religious exercises in public assemblies should have this goal: Let everything be done to edify. As for speaking in an unknown tongue, if someone were present who could interpret, two miraculous gifts might be exercised at once, thereby benefiting the church and confirming the faith of the listeners at the same time. As for prophesying, only two or three should speak at one meeting, and this one after another, not all at once. The person who is inspired by the Spirit of God will observe order and decency in delivering his revelations. God never teaches people to neglect their duties or to act in any way unbecoming their age or position.

When the apostle encourages Christian women to seek information on religious subjects from their husbands at home, it shows that believing families ought to gather together to promote spiritual knowledge. The Spirit of Christ can never contradict itself; and if their revelations are against those of the apostle, they do not come from the same Spirit. The way to keep peace, truth, and order in the church is to seek what is good for it, to tolerate that which is not harmful to its welfare, and to maintain proper behavior, order, and decency.
